title: RNNS, LSTMS & GRUS
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real lecturer-quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 7 (Recurrence)

sections.title: AN RNN IS A LOOP WITH MEMORY
sections.exam: Working principle: a loop feeds the hidden state h back in each step, so information persists across the sequence.
sections.ascii:
x1 → x2 → x3        (inputs over time)
 │     │     │
 ▼     ▼     ▼
[h1]→[h2]→[h3]       h = hidden state = running memory
 │     │     │
 ▼     ▼     ▼
 y1    y2    y3       (outputs)

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: The same cell is copied at every step; the horizontal arrows carry the hidden state forward. That loop is the 'working principle' answer.

sections.title: THE ARCHITECTURE ZOO
sections.exam: Pick the I/O shape by task: encoder–decoder (seq2seq) is the shape for translation, summarisation and parsing.
sections.ascii:
one-to-many        many-to-one        many-to-many(sync)     encoder→decoder
  in                in in in            in in in              in in in ┊
   ↓                 ↓  ↓  ↓             ↓  ↓  ↓               ↘  ↓  ↙ ┊ ↓ ↓ ↓
 [h]→[h]→[h]        [h]→[h]→[h]         [h]→[h]→[h]           [h]→[h]→[h]┊[h]→[h]→[h]
  ↓   ↓   ↓               ↓             ↓   ↓   ↓                       ┊ ↓  ↓  ↓
 out out out             out           out out out                     ┊out out out
 (music from mood)  (sentiment/author) (tag each word)        (translate / summarise)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: A whole input is read; only the final hidden vector is kept as a summary. The 'many-to-one' shape — e.g. sentiment or author prediction.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: From one summary vector the network emits an output each step — 'one-to-many', e.g. generating music from a single mood label.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Encoder compresses the whole input, then the decoder generates the whole output — the shape tied to translation, summarisation and parsing.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Drawn step by step, so you can see where the encoder's summary hands over to the decoder.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Each decoder step produces a token and passes its state on — how the output sequence is built one item at a time.

sections.title: TRAINING: BPTT & VANISHING GRADIENT
sections.exam: BPTT gradients are w.r.t. the SHARED recurrent weights; fix vanishing gradients with clipping, gating (LSTM/GRU) or attention — NOT dropout.
sections.ascii:
loss J
   ▲  multiply by small numbers at every hop ↓
[h1]←[h2]←[h3]← ... ←[hT]
  └─ gradient shrinks → ~0 ── early steps stop learning (the 'fading telephone game')

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The loss gradient propagates back along the unrolled chain. The gradients are w.r.t. the shared recurrent weights, summed over all steps.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Multiplying small numbers at each step shrinks the gradient toward zero, so the earliest steps barely learn — distant events can't be linked.

sections.title: LSTM — THREE GATES
sections.exam: LSTM = forget+input+output gates over a protected cell state; the gates use sigmoid, the candidate uses tanh.
sections.ascii:
┌─ forget σ ─┐  drop irrelevant old memory
 C(t-1) ──┤             ├──► C(t)
          ├─ input  σ ─┤  write new (candidate = tanh)
 x,h ─────┤             │
          └─ output σ ─┘  → h(t) read out to next step

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Three gates guard a protected cell state that runs straight through — a clear highway for gradients. Gates are sigmoids; the candidate is tanh.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The lecturer's view of the same cell — the three gate units feeding the cell state.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Each gate f, i, o is a sigmoid of the inputs; the candidate uses tanh — confirms 'gates = sigmoid, candidate = tanh'.

sections.title: GRU — TWO GATES + THE CROSSFADE
sections.exam: GRU = reset+update gates; h_t = (1−z)·h_(t-1) + z·h̃_t blends old vs new — the carry-over that beats vanishing gradients.
sections.ascii:
h_t = (1 − z) · h_(t-1)   +   z · h̃_t
          └─ keep OLD ─┘        └─ take NEW ─┘

 z ≈ 0 :  ██████████░░░░   mostly OLD memory  (smooth carry-over → beats vanishing gradient)
 z ≈ 1 :  ░░░░██████████   mostly NEW candidate
 reset r ≈ 0 : ignore the past entirely (e.g. a fresh sentence)

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Only two gates — reset r and update z — and no separate cell state. Simpler than the LSTM, often just as good.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Shows the update gate z blending the previous hidden state with the new candidate.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: r and z are sigmoids; the candidate uses the reset-gated previous state; the final state is the (1−z)/z blend.

sections.title: SEQ2SEQ & THE EXAM'S QUIET CHOICES
sections.exam: Match last layer + loss to the task; teacher forcing feeds the real previous token during training.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The encoder turns the input into a context vector; the decoder unrolls that into the output sequence.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Bridges sequence models to embeddings — how tokens become the vectors an RNN consumes.
